text: Welcome to this video all about the setup and port placement of the DaVinci XI for robotic thoracic surgery.
First of all, I just want to highlight how much of a team activity this is.
This is Kiri, my scrub nurse, setting up and draping the XI.
It is so much easier than the third generation and they click on really easily.
I've got my dual console and Kiri's got the six ports all laid out.
We've got four 8mm ports and we've got two 12mm ports.
We've got our tip-up grasper, Cadia and Maryland.
And obviously the two 12mm ports, we've got downsizers so that we can put this 8mm port in if we want to use instruments, and then we take that out if we want to use the 12mm.
I always use a retractor just in case of emergencies, and we always practice emergencies, but that's another thing.
So this is a patient having a left-sided lobectomy.
I like to angle the patient about 20, 30 degrees up just to get that diaphragm down.
I think the camera placement is absolutely the most important.
I like to be at the top of the chest.
I like to be above the ninth rib.
That's about the eighth space.
I like to give large amounts of local anesthetic for every port placement.
We use a quarter percent bupivacaine with adrenaline, 60 mils.
and dilute that down to 150 ml total so we can give large volume.
So we just do a little bit of diathermy and I like to do a direct under vision entry with this allied medical port using CO2 at the tip.
You can watch yourself go into the chest, you can see the lung, you can see the CO2 blowing the lung away and you're safely in the chest.
The reason that's better than using some mosquitoes or a lot of diathermy is that that causes a lot of bleeding and oozing and it drips down the port, whereas this prevents dripping a lot.
I'm just looking to check that I can see around the chest beautifully because I can easily change my level at this point, but I'm very happy with the port placement.
I can see the top of the chest and the bottom of the chest.
